definition: *Eldritch* means weird, uncanny, otherworldly — belonging to a realm outside the ordinary and slightly wrong for it. It carries a flavor no synonym quite matches: not merely strange but strange as if from elsewhere, a thing that does not sit right in this world.
history: Probably from Old English *el-*, 'foreign, other,' plus *rīce*, 'realm, kingdom' — literally 'of the otherworldly kingdom.' The word survived chiefly in Scots and northern English, a quiet regional relic for the eerie and the elf-touched. Its defining turn came in the 20th century, when H. P. Lovecraft seized on *eldritch* as a keyword of cosmic horror and fixed its modern sense for good. It reaches Magic: The Gathering — in the Eldrazi and the set Eldritch Moon — already soaked in that Lovecraftian charge.

etymology.origin: Prob. Old English el- 'foreign, other' + rīce 'realm' — 'of an otherworldly kingdom'
etymology.note: A Scots survival Lovecraft launched into cosmic horror. It means, more or less, 'from the other kingdom'.

cards.name: Eldritch Pact
cards.set_name: Commander Legends: Battle for Baldur's Gate

flavor_snippets.html: "The ravings of the mad are laced with <mark>eldritch</mark> knowledge."
Its tines cannot be rung by mere physical force. Only magic of great power can start its <mark>eldritch</mark> resonance.

mechanics_snippets.html: Search your library for a creature card with mana value X or less, where X is 2 plus the sacrificed creature's mana value. Put that card onto the battlefield, then shuffle. Exile <mark>Eldritch</mark> Evolution.
